array.title: Array
array.whatIsIt: An array is a list of items stored right next to each other in memory. Every item has a number, called an index, starting at 0.
array.whyUseIt: Because every item has a fixed spot, you can jump straight to any item instantly instead of hunting for it. That makes arrays the go-to choice whenever you need fast lookups.
array.analogy: Think of a row of numbered lockers. If someone tells you 'locker 7', you walk straight to it - you don't check lockers 1 through 6 first.

types.name: Static array
types.description: Its size is locked in from the start. Very fast, but it can't grow.

types.name: Dynamic array
types.description: Grows automatically when it runs out of room. This is what std::vector does in C++.

array.visualAlt: Five boxes in a row labeled with indexes 0 through 4
interviewQuestions: Reverse an array without using extra space.
Find the largest sum you can get from a group of neighboring numbers (Kadane's algorithm).
Find the missing number in a list that should contain 1 to n.
Rotate an array to the left or right by k positions.

stack.title: Stack
stack.kicker: Last one in is the first one out
stack.whatIsIt: A stack only lets you add or remove items from one single end, called the top. You can't reach into the middle.
stack.whyUseIt: Stacks are perfect whenever the most recent thing needs to be handled first - like the undo button in a text editor, or how a program remembers which function called which.
stack.analogy: A stack of plates on a table. You place a new plate on top, and when you need a plate, you take the top one. You never pull one out from the bottom.

types.name: Array-based stack
types.description: Built on top of an array or vector.

types.name: Linked-list-based stack
types.description: Every push or pop just adds/removes a node at the front of a chain.

stack.visualAlt: A vertical stack of blocks with the top block highlighted
interviewQuestions: Check whether the brackets in a string are balanced.
Build a queue using only two stacks.
Evaluate a postfix math expression.
Design a stack that can tell you its minimum value in O(1) time.

queue.title: Queue
queue.kicker: First one in is the first one out
queue.whatIsIt: A queue adds new items at the back and removes items from the front - the exact opposite ends.
queue.whyUseIt: Queues are the natural fit whenever things need to be handled in the order they arrived, like a print queue or a list of requests waiting to be processed.
queue.analogy: A line at a coffee shop. Whoever joined the line first gets served first, and every new person joins at the back.

types.name: Simple queue
types.description: Strict first-come, first-served order.

types.name: Circular queue
types.description: Wraps back around to the start so freed-up space gets reused.

types.name: Priority queue
types.description: Items leave based on importance, not on arrival order.

queue.visualAlt: A row of blocks with an arrow entering at the back and leaving at the front
interviewQuestions: Build a queue using only two stacks.
Design a circular queue from scratch.
Build a priority queue without using a library.
Find the first character in a stream that never repeats.

string.title: String
string.whatIsIt: A string is just text: a sequence of characters sitting next to each other in memory, like a name or a sentence.
string.whyUseIt: Almost every program handles text somewhere - names, messages, file paths - and strings are how that text gets stored, searched, and changed.
string.analogy: A word written on a strip of paper, one letter per box. You can point at any letter using its position, just like an array.

operations.name: Get a piece of the string

types.name: Immutable string
types.description: Can't be changed after it's created - editing it actually creates a brand new string.

types.name: Mutable string
types.description: Can be edited in place without creating a new copy. C++'s std::string works this way.

string.visualAlt: Characters H, E, L, L, O stored in consecutive indexed boxes
interviewQuestions: Reverse a string in place.
Check whether a string reads the same backward as forward.
Find the first character that never repeats.
Check whether two strings are anagrams of each other.

linked-list.title: Linked List
linked-list.whatIsIt: Instead of sitting side by side like an array, each item (called a node) lives on its own and simply points to the next one in line.
linked-list.whyUseIt: Because nothing needs to be next to anything else, you can add or remove an item without shifting every other item over - something arrays can't do cheaply.
linked-list.analogy: A treasure hunt: each clue tells you where to find the next one. You can't skip ahead - you must follow the chain one link at a time.

types.name: Singly linked list
types.description: Each node points forward to the next node only.

types.name: Doubly linked list
types.description: Each node points both forward and backward.

types.name: Circular linked list
types.description: The last node loops back and points to the first one.

linked-list.visualAlt: Boxes connected by arrows, each pointing to the next box
interviewQuestions: Reverse a linked list.
Detect whether a linked list loops back on itself (Floyd's algorithm).
Find the middle node while only passing through the list once.
Merge two already-sorted linked lists into one.

tree.title: Tree
tree.whatIsIt: A tree starts with one node at the top (the root) and branches downward, where every node can have children of its own.
tree.whyUseIt: Trees are a natural fit for anything with a hierarchy, and a sorted tree lets you search, insert, and delete in a fraction of the time a plain list would take.
tree.analogy: A family tree. Everyone has one parent (except the very first ancestor), and each parent can have several children - the structure branches out.

types.name: Binary Tree
types.description: Each node has at most two children.

types.name: Binary Search Tree (BST)
types.description: Smaller values go left, bigger values go right - this ordering is what makes searching fast.

types.name: AVL Tree
types.description: A BST that automatically keeps itself balanced so it never gets lopsided.

types.name: Red-Black Tree
types.description: Another self-balancing BST, using node 'colors' as the balancing rule.

types.name: B-Tree
types.description: A tree where each node can hold several values at once - common in databases.

tree.visualAlt: A root node connected to child nodes forming a hierarchical tree structure.
interviewQuestions: Implement inorder, preorder, and postorder traversal.
Find the height of a binary tree.
Check whether a tree is balanced.
Find the lowest common ancestor of two nodes.

deque.title: Deque
deque.kicker: A queue open at both ends
deque.whatIsIt: Short for 'double-ended queue' - unlike a normal queue, you can add or remove items from both the front and the back.
deque.whyUseIt: That extra flexibility makes deques handy for undo/redo history, browser back-and-forward navigation, and sliding-window problems where you need to drop items from either end quickly.
deque.analogy: A train where passengers can board or get off from either the front door or the back door - not just one end.

types.name: Input-restricted deque
types.description: You can only add items at one end, but remove from both.

types.name: Output-restricted deque
types.description: You can only remove items from one end, but add at both.

deque.visualAlt: A deque showing insertion and deletion possible from both the front and rear.
interviewQuestions: Implement a deque using a plain array.
Implement a deque using a doubly linked list.
Solve the sliding window maximum problem.
Design a browser history feature using a deque.

hash-table.title: Hash Table
hash-table.kicker: Look things up by name, not position
hash-table.whatIsIt: A hash table stores data as key-value pairs - like a name paired with a phone number - and uses a hash function to figure out exactly where to store or find each pair.
hash-table.whyUseIt: Because the hash function tells you almost exactly where to look, hash tables give near-instant lookups, insertions, and deletions - perfect for dictionaries and caches.
hash-table.analogy: A library where every book has a code printed on the spine. Instead of scanning every shelf, the code tells you exactly which shelf to walk to.

types.name: Separate chaining
types.description: When two keys land on the same spot, they're stored together in a small linked list at that spot.

types.name: Open addressing
types.description: When a spot is taken, the table looks for the next free spot nearby instead.

hash-table.visualAlt: A hash function mapping keys to indexed buckets in a hash table.
interviewQuestions: Implement a hash table from scratch.
Explain a couple of ways to handle collisions.
Find the first character in a string that never repeats.
Design a Least Recently Used (LRU) cache.

heap.title: Heap
heap.kicker: The most important item is always on top
heap.whatIsIt: A heap is a tree-shaped structure where every parent follows one rule versus its children: always bigger (a Max Heap) or always smaller (a Min Heap).
heap.whyUseIt: Whenever you repeatedly need the biggest or smallest item out of a changing collection - like a priority queue or a scheduler - a heap gets you that answer fast, every time.
heap.analogy: A company org chart where the CEO always sits at the top. Every manager outranks their own team, so the top authority is always easy to spot.

operations.name: Build a heap from a list

types.name: Max Heap
types.description: Every parent is greater than or equal to its children.

types.name: Min Heap
types.description: Every parent is smaller than or equal to its children.

types.name: Binary Heap
types.description: The most common way to build a heap, using a complete binary tree.

heap.visualAlt: A complete binary tree illustrating the heap property with parent nodes greater than or less than their children.
interviewQuestions: Implement a Max Heap from scratch.
Turn a Min Heap into a Max Heap.
Find the kth largest value using a heap.
Explain how Heap Sort works.

graph.title: Graph
graph.whatIsIt: A graph is a set of nodes (called vertices) connected by links (called edges). Unlike a tree, a graph can loop back on itself and connect nodes in any pattern.
graph.whyUseIt: Graphs are how we model real networks - friend connections on social media, roads on a map, or links between web pages - anywhere relationships matter more than a strict hierarchy.
graph.analogy: A map of cities connected by roads. Each city is a vertex, each road is an edge, and there can be several different routes between any two cities.

types.name: Undirected graph
types.description: Edges go both ways - if A connects to B, B connects to A.

types.name: Directed graph
types.description: Edges point in one direction only, like a one-way street.

types.name: Weighted graph
types.description: Each edge carries a cost, like distance or travel time.

types.name: Unweighted graph
types.description: Edges just represent a connection, with no extra cost attached.

types.name: Acyclic graph
types.description: Contains no loops. A Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) is common in task scheduling.

graph.visualAlt: A collection of vertices connected by edges representing relationships in a graph.
interviewQuestions: Implement Breadth-First Search (BFS).
Implement Depth-First Search (DFS).
Detect whether a graph contains a cycle.
Find the shortest path between two vertices.
Explain the difference between an adjacency list and an adjacency matrix.
